So the short version is: extractors are still very much alive, but they now provide only the structured “conversation turns” lane. The newer subtree-first system handles the broader local visible context, and prompt assembly merges both, with reply mode preferring extractor output and continuation mode preferring subtree XML.
yeah so we have per-app extractors as well as a fallback extractor? how does the fallback extractor work
Yes. The fallback path is actually two layers:
. A lot of the app-specific extractors also fall back to that same extractor if their app-specific parsing fails.

is the “smart generic” fallback. It assumes the AX tree looks like many modern chat UIs: message content groups interleaved with action bars like “Copy”, “Reply”, “React”, “Feedback”, etc. That logic starts in
It recursively searches for a container that has at least 2 child groups that look like message-action groups (
A child counts as an “action group” only if its subrole is
or it contains buttons whose descriptions contain those keywords
Once it finds such a container, it walks its direct children and treats each action group as a delimiter between messages. Everything before an action group is the message body for one turn. That’s
To label the speaker, it checks whether that action group contains assistant-like cues such as
. If yes, the turn is labeled
To get the text, it just runs
across the pending content groups and joins the results in
If that doesn’t find anything usable, it falls back again to
is much dumber but very broad. It just recursively walks the tree looking for any
. A node counts as a message if it has at least two text-ish children and one looks like a short speaker name (
chars) while another looks like a real body (
App-specific extractor: “I know this app’s AX structure.”
: “I don’t know the app, but I recognize modern chat UI action bars.”
: “I don’t even recognize action bars, but this subtree still looks like speaker + message text.”

One practical note: the fallback path is deliberately structural, not semantic. It doesn’t know special app behaviors like Claude’s “Thought process” section, so app-specific extractors are better whenever they work.
can you rename this branch custom extractor flows
I normalized your requested name to a valid git branch name by replacing spaces with hyphens.

So one of the things that we support right now is basically capturing AX fixtures or dumps and then designing custom extractors based on the XML information that we have. However, that code is a little bit outdated, I think. I'm not positive. We were sort of on a mission to move away from app-specific extractors entirely and focus instead on a generic extractor that can properly basically use heuristics to navigate the AX tree. However, that has proven to be a little bit ineffective. So for now, I want to go back to our approach of custom per-app extractors, but we need to first make sure that we're in a good spot. So I wanna make sure that we're able to, first of all, capture dumps, like comprehensive dumps from apps that we want to have custom extractors for. Then, using these dumps, I want to analyze the structure of the AX tree so we can design, you know, flexible solutions, flexible and tailored solutions that can identify, for example, important content and filter out unrelated information, things like conversation turns, etc. And then finally, I want to be able to use these raw captured dumps as backtesting, basically as benchmarks to see if our context processing is doing properly, okay? So let's do this.
I set up the fixture workflow so we can lean back into app-specific extractors without fighting stale tooling.
The core change is a shared normalization layer in
. It loads both raw AX captures and replay-session dumps into one shape, reconstructs focused input state when older artifacts only have
, and lets extractor replay run against serialized trees. On top of that,
APIs again, so offline replay/backtesting works instead of crashing on the old
I also added a new analyzer at

. Given any saved fixture, it now reports extractor selection, extracted turns, subtree output, text-rich container candidates, and text-node samples, which should make custom extractor design much more grounded. New raw captures from
and carry capture settings too. I documented the new workflow in
